figures.caption: Figure 6: Qualitative comparison of physical plausibility on RICH [11]. We compare Human3R [4] and UniCon3R against ground truth on a streaming sequence. Yellow boxes highlight floating or implausible 4D reconstructions of Human3R compared to the more grounded predictions of UniCon3R.

Figure 1: Visually plausible reconstructions can still be physically ungrounded. Human3R [4] jointly reconstructs humans and scenes, but can produce misaligned bodies and scene interpenetration. UniCon3R predicts dense per-vertex contact (green inset) and feeds it back into reconstruction, improving body-scene alignment over time.

Figure 5: Qualitative comparison of local human mesh recovery. We compare Human3R∗ [4] and UniCon3R against ground truth. UniCon3R produces body pose and scene alignment that are closer to ground truth, particularly in cases with clear scene support.

Table 3: Ablation study on RICH [11]. We report global motion and physical plausibility metrics while progressively adding each UniCon3R component. Human3R∗: released checkpoint in our pipeline; WA-MPJPE and W-MPJPE are in mm. Lower is better.

Figure 3: Local human mesh reconstruction on SLOPER4D [6] and 3DPW [32]. We report PVE and MPJPE in mm. Human3R∗ denotes the released checkpoint evaluated in our pipeline, and Human3R† denotes the same architecture fine-tuned with our training protocol. On SLOPER4D, we additionally report maximum penetration depth, Pen. Max, in cm. Lower is better for all metrics.

Figure 7: Qualitative comparison of contact prediction on web videos. We compare dense pervertex binary contact predictions from UniCon3R against DECO [28] on in-the-wild web videos. Contact vertices are shown in green on the mesh surface.

Figure 4: Qualitative comparison of global human motion estimation on EMDB-2 [14]. We compare Human3R∗ and UniCon3R against ground truth after world-coordinate alignment. Two viewpoints of the same sequence are shown.

Table 2: Contact estimation and physical grounding on RICH [11]. Left: binary SMPL-vertex contact prediction using precision, recall, F1, and geometric contact error (Geo., cm). Right: physical grounding on reconstructed RICH sequences. Human3R∗ denotes the released checkpoint evaluated in our pipeline, and Human3R† denotes the same architecture trained with our protocol. Following HuMoS [30], we estimate a robust ground height and use a 5 mm tolerance to report Collision Ratio (Coll., %), Penetrate (Pen., cm), Float (cm), and Pen. Max (P.Max, cm).

Figure 2: Overview of UniCon3R. Left: At time t, the Scene-Human branch encodes the current frame into image tokens Ft and a human prompt Ht, which interact with the previous state St−1 through the frozen 4D decoders to update the persistent state St and predict scene, camera, and human parameters. UniCon3R further introduces a contact branch which outputs dense vertex-level contact on SMPL [20] mesh. Top right: UniCon3R constructs a scene-aware contact prompt Ct (§4.1) from scene context, explicit metric geometry, and a temporal prior. The contact prompt is decoded by the same frozen 4D decoders, yielding a refined contact token C′ t. Bottom right: after decoding, the refined contact token C′ t predicts dense SMPL contact and drives contact-guided latent refinement (§4.2) by producing a residual update to the human latent before final SMPL-X regression. Thus, unlike parallel contact readout, UniCon3R uses contact inside the reconstruction pathway to improve the final body estimate.
